title: Ukraine Removes Military Commander Syrskyi, Appoints Drapatyi
dek: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky removed Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief and appointed Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, to lead the armed forces. The move came under public pressure and marks the third command change since Russia's 2022 invasion.
author: The GoCheckMyNews Desk
key_fact: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as Ukraine's military commander-in-chief, marking the third such leadership change since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022.
bottom_line: Drapatyi's appointment marks a departure in leadership style and a bet on military accountability, but the constraints are real: dependence on Western supply, political pressure from above, and public expectations that may outpace deliverable results. Watch for his first operational decisions and whether military morale and soldier safety metrics improve. The true test will be whether his command philosophy translates into tactical results on the battlefield and whether public support, now enthusiastic, endures through the inevitable next setback.
body: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky removed Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of the armed forces and appointed Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, to the post, according to BBC reporting. Drapatyi becomes the third commander-in-chief since Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022.

Drapatyi's appointment came after public pressure and protests in which his name was chanted by soldiers and civilians demanding Syrskyi's dismissal. Per BBC reporting, the nomination has been welcomed by both civil society and the military, marking a rare moment of consensus in a conflict that has strained Ukrainian society.

Drapatyi graduated from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004 and began his military career as a lieutenant. In May 2014, during pro-Russian separatist activity in the Donbas, his battalion entered the southern city of Mariupol to free hostages held in the city's police department. Later that year, he was commended for withdrawing encircled Ukrainian units from a border area of the Luhansk region, according to the BBC account. When Russia's full-scale invasion began in 2022, Drapatyi served as deputy commander of joint forces. He led operational groups in several areas of the front line, successfully pushing back Russian advances toward Kryvyi Rih and liberating settlements in the Kherson and Dnipropretrovsk regions. By 2024, he was heading the land troops and commanding task forces in the east's most intense combat areas.

In 2025, a Russian missile struck a training ground in the Dnipropetrovsk region under Drapatyi's command, killing 12 soldiers and wounding 60 others. Drapatyi resigned the same day, accepting personal responsibility for what he called a conspiracy of silence and impunity. He wrote: An army in which commanders bear personal responsibility for the lives of personnel keeps living. An army in which nobody is responsible for a loss dies. Zelensky later appointed him commander of the Joint Forces, a move widely praised. From that appointment, tensions with Syrskyi grew, per BBC reporting, though the substance of those tensions is not fully detailed.

Military analysts and observers view the leadership change with cautious optimism tempered by realism about constraints ahead. Mykola Bielieskov, senior analyst at the Ukrainian NGO Come Back Alive, stated per the BBC: Drapatyi has a very good reputation among the rank and file, especially those who joined after 2022 as civilians. Bielieskov also noted that Drapatyi will operate under serious constraints: The Commander-in-Chief has a lot of leverage. But war is a combination of government, the president's office, military suppliers, dependence on Western partners, so he's got a tricky path before him.

Many observers characterize Syrskyi's approach as outdated, placing little value on human life in favor of brutal offensives, according to BBC reporting. Drapatyi's appointment is viewed by some as a potential shift toward a different operational philosophy. In his first message after appointment as Commander-in-Chief, Drapatyi promised to work without making loud promises, with responsibility for our decisions, and with respect for those on the front line, per the BBC account.

But warnings about the difficulty of the role are explicit. Valery Zaluzhnyi, the previous army chief removed in 2024, predicted that Drapatyi would have a very difficult time, much harder than he imagines. Former interior ministry adviser Oleksiy Kopytko noted that as commander-in-chief, you are worshipped and loved by all today, not loved by everyone tomorrow, and are guilty of everything the day after tomorrow, with none of your achievements mattering. Bielieskov warned against oversimplification that can lead to demands for sweeping improvements in unrealistic timeframes.

Drapatyi faces immediate priorities: mobilizing manpower, tackling corruption in conscription and procurement, and sustaining operations against Russia with no clear path to ceasefire. Prospects for a ceasefire remain slim and Ukraine faces another harsh winter of war, per BBC reporting. When he resigned from his Ground Forces position in 2025, Drapatyi listed issues he had tackled, from combat training reform to unfair mobilization and flawed procurement, stating: There is expected resistance, but efforts need to be stepped up.
